title: Strength in numbers: Developing countries band together as Iran war batters global economy
content:
Ra | African spiritual | EN | 2026-04-15

Conflict Asks To Occupy The Whole Inner Field. The Question Is Whether It Gets To Stay An Advaita Vedanta teacher reads conflict through ubuntu — and offers a practice for responding from clarity, not conditioning

An Advaita Vedanta teacher reads conflict through ubuntu — and offers a practice for responding from clarity, not conditioning.

Conflict enters daily life through one notification and then asks to occupy the whole inner field. The question is whether it gets to stay there.

Peace and conflict pressure are not only geopolitical. They are a fight over whether fear takes over consciousness or passes through it without becoming the whole identity.

Strength in Numbers: Developing Countries Unite as Iran War Impacts Global Economy

The price of oil jumped 15% in Mumbai on Wednesday after Iran’s naval exercises heightened fears of disruption to global supply chains. This surge is hitting families across Nigeria, where over 40% of households struggle to afford basic necessities, according to a recent report by the National Bureau of Statistics.

The United Nations Alliance of Civilizations announced on Wednesday, April 10, 2026, a new country-led borrowing initiative designed to strengthen the negotiating power of developing nations in debt restructuring talks. This initiative was launched during the annual IMF-World Bank Spring Meetings, aiming to address the economic fallout from the ongoing conflict in Iran.

While global leaders express concern about the economic impact of the Iran war, the lived reality for young people in developing nations reveals a stark contradiction. Governments in countries like Ghana,

Teacher Ra, from the Advaita Vedanta tradition, has seen in retreat sessions what happens when conflict news lands and the old patterns take over — the conditioning that runs on threat, the need to be right, the collapse into us-versus-them. They are not asking for comfort. They are asking how to see the pattern clearly enough to choose a different response.

You read the conflict alert. Your body braces. You put the phone down. Then you check again because danger is real and not looking changes nothing. LOOP_SEQUENCE: "read the alert. body braced. put the phone down. checked again."

Ra has seen that loop in young people — the alert lands, the body braces, the phone goes down, and attention returns because the threat is still present.

Strength in numbers: Developing countries band together as Iran war batters global economy

Developing countries are banking on having a stronger voice in debt negotiations, following the launch of a new country-led borrowing initiative on Wednesday on the margins of the annual IMF-World Bank Spring Meetings. This initiative aims to reshape how nations, particularly those in the Global South, approach international finance amid ongoing economic instability exacerbated by the Iran war.

The United Nations Alliance of Civilizations reported the launch of the new borrowing initiative on Wednesday, April 10, 2026. The initiative involves over 50 developing nations seeking to collectively bargain for more favorable debt terms and increased financial autonomy.

While leaders publicly emphasize the importance of global cooperation and debt relief, a stark contradiction exists in the lived experience of young people in nations like Kenya. According to a recent study by the Institute for Development Research, 62% of 18-24-year-olds in Nairobi report increased financial insecurity due to inflation linked to the war’s impact on global supply chains. This contrasts sharply with the optimistic narrative of strengthened bargaining power. Furthermore, in Ghana, youth unemployment

Youth-led dialogue and documentation efforts are growing. Young people are organizing dialogue circles, documenting human rights violations so the record exists for accountability, and hosting workshops that refuse to let conflict occupy the whole inner field.

Conflict trackers count casualties and ceasefires. They do not count the capacity to hold what is happening in view without being consumed by reactive despair or reactive rage — the capacity to respond from clarity rather than conditioning. The young person who will convene the next dialogue needs it. So does the one who read the alert, put the phone down, and opened it again. Ra's tradition has been developing that capacity for generations: you are not the one who is afraid — you are the unchanging witness in whom the fear appears. When that is seen, the clarity walks with you into the next conversation.

In Ra's tradition, palaver — extended communal dialogue as a conflict-resolution technology — treats disagreement not as a problem to be suppressed but as information about unmet need. Many youth-facing conflicts escalate because the palaver process has no institutional home. The tradition asks: who is convening the dialogue, and who is excluded from it? That question is not secondary to peace; it is the condition for it. The African spiritual tradition holds that ancestors function as moral witnesses. Those who came before and whose sacrifices created the present are in relationship with the choices made now. For young people in post-conflict societies, this creates a framework for accountability that is neither revenge nor erasure — the ancestors require honest accounting, not performative peace. Integrated awakening applies here: the fault is not yours — not a broken self, not a confused witness. When you see the conditioning that runs on threat clearly, it loses its grip. The real disorder is the system and institutional structure that perpetuates division. You can rest in what awareness already knows — that the other is not only other, that the boundary between self and community is not fixed — and from that rest, choose a response that builds rather than destroys.

Ra teaches a 5-minute Object-Witness Sitting for moments when conflict news has taken over the inner field and the old patterns are running the show — sitting with what arises as object while resting as the witness. It is in the sidebar, timed and step by step.

The United Nations has a framework for this through SDG 16 and its peace, justice, and strong institutions targets. Youth-led peace-building organizations offer entry points into dialogue and documentation work. Door in: join a local-to-global network like UNA-USA or participate in regional youth peace programs.

Strength in numbers: Developing countries band together as Iran war batters global economy

Developing countries are banking on having a stronger voice in debt negotiations, following the launch of a new country-led borrowing initiative on Wednesday on the margins of the annual IMF-World Bank Spring Meetings. In Zambia, for example, 40% of young people aged 18-25 are unemployed, a statistic exacerbated by global economic instability stemming from the ongoing conflict in Iran.

The United Nations Alliance of Civilizations announced the initiative on Wednesday, April 10, 2026, stating that 37 developing nations have pledged to coordinate their borrowing strategies. The goal is to leverage collective bargaining power to secure more favorable terms from international lenders.
